import processing
from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer

def translate_geometry():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    # Load the shapefile as a vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Layer', 'ogr')

    # Run the Translate Geometry algorithm
    result = processing.run('native:translategeometry', {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'DELTA_X': 10.0,
        'DELTA_Y': 20.0,
        'DELTA_Z': 0.0,
        'DELTA_M': 0.0,
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    })

    # Load the translated layer to QGIS
    translated_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Translated Layer', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(translated_layer)

# Execute the function
translate_geometry()

brief_description: Performs algebraic operations using raster layers and generates in-memory result.
full_description:
Performs algebraic operations using raster layers and generates in-memory result.
The resulting layer will have its values computed according to an expression. The expression can contain numerical values, operators and references to any of the layers in the current project.
A virtual raster layer is a raster layer defined by its URI and whose pixels are calculated on-the-fly. It’s not a new file on disk; the virtual layer is still connected to the rasters used in the calculation meaning that deleting or moving these rasters would break it. A Layer name can be provided, otherwise the calculation expression is used as such. Removing the virtual layer from the project deletes it, and it can be made persistent in file using the layer Export ► Save as… contextual menu.

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layers
parameters.description: List of input raster layers.

parameters.name: EXPRESSION
parameters.label: Expression
parameters.description: Raster-based expression that will be used to calculate the output raster layer..

parameters.name: EXTENT
parameters.label: Output extent Optional
parameters.description: Specify the spatial extent of the output raster layer.If the extent is not specified, the minimum extent that coversall the selected reference layers will be used. Available methods are: Calculate from layer…: uses extent of a layer loaded in the current project Calculate from layout map…: uses exte

parameters.name: CELLSIZE
parameters.label: Output cell size (leave empty to set automatically) Optional
parameters.description: Cell size of the output raster layer.If the cell size is not specified, the minimum cell size ofthe selected reference layer(s) will be used.The cell size will be the same for the X and Y axes..

parameters.name: CRS
parameters.label: Output CRS Optional
parameters.description: CRS of the output raster layer.If the output CRS is not specified, the CRS of the firstreference layer will be used..

parameters.name: LAYER_NAME
parameters.label: Output layer name Optional
parameters.description: The name to assign to the generated layer.If not set, the text of the calculation expression is used..

use_cases: Clipping national datasets to study area extents
Mosaicking tiled raster datasets (DEM tiles, NLCD, etc.)
Raster preprocessing for image analysis pipelines
Format conversion for interoperability with other systems
original_code_example:
import processing
from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsRasterLayer

def virtual_raster_calc():
    input_layers = ['path/to/raster1.tif', 'path/to/raster2.tif']  # List of raster paths
    output_path = 'path/to/output_raster.tif'
    
    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_layers,
        'EXPRESSION': 'raster1@1 + raster2@1',  # Example expression using raster band 1
        'EXTENT': '16.0, 17.0, 45.0, 46.0',  # xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax
        'CELLSIZE': 10.0,  # Cell size in units of raster CRS
        'CRS': 'EPSG:4326',  # Coordinate reference system
        'LAYER_NAME': 'Calculation Result',  # Optional layer name
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    
    result = processing.run('native:virtualrastercalc', parameters)
    
    output_layer = QgsRasterLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Virtual Raster Calculation')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_layer)

# Execute the function
virtual_raster_calc()

brief_description: Takes a point layer and generates a polygon layer containing the Voronoi polygons (known also as Thiessen polygons) corresponding to those input points.
full_description:
Takes a point layer and generates a polygon layer containing the Voronoi polygons (known also as Thiessen polygons) corresponding to those input points.
Any location within a Voronoi polygon is closer to the associated point than to any other point.
Default menu: Vector ► Geometry Tools

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: Input point vector layer.

parameters.name: BUFFER
parameters.label: Buffer region (% of extent)
parameters.description: The extent of the output layer will be this muchbigger than the extent of the input layer.

parameters.name: TOLERANCE
parameters.label: Tolerance Optional
parameters.description: Specifies an optional snapping tolerance which can be used to improve the robustness of the voronoi..

parameters.name: COPY_ATTRIBUTES
parameters.label: Copy attributes from input features
parameters.description: Specifies whether fields storing involved point features ID should be added to the output.If False, an id field is created to identify the polygons..

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Voronoi polygons
parameters.description: Specify the output layer (with the Voronoi polygons).One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

use_cases: Spatial selection and attribute-based filtering
Overlay analysis for multi-criteria site suitability
Data preparation and cleaning for GIS workflows
Geometry repair and validation before spatial joins
original_code_example:
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def create_voronoi_polygons():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    # Load the point vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Points', 'ogr')

    # Run the Voronoi polygons algorithm
    result = processing.run('native:voronoipolygons', {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'BUFFER': 0.0,  # Default value
        'TOLERANCE': 0.0,  # Default value
        'COPY_ATTRIBUTES': True,  # Default value
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    })

    # Load the Voronoi polygons layer to QGIS
    voronoi_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Voronoi Polygons', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(voronoi_layer)

# Execute the function
create_voronoi_polygons()

Creates wedge shaped buffers from input points.

parameters.name: META_VERSION

parameters.name: META_TYPE

parameters.name: META_CENTER

brief_description: Performs a validity check on the geometries of a vector layer.
full_description:
Performs a validity check on the geometries of a vector layer.
The geometries are classified in three groups (valid, invalid and error) and for each group, a vector layer with its features is generated:
The attribute tables of the generated layers will contain some additional information (“message” for the error layer, “FID” and “_errors” for the invalid layer and only “FID” for the valid layer):
The attribute table of each generated vector layer will contain some additional information (number of errors found and types of error):
Default menu: Vector ► Geometry Tools

# Import necessary modules
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def check_layer_validity():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    valid_output_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    invalid_output_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    error_output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    # Load the shapefile as a vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Layer', 'ogr')

    # Run the Validity Check algorithm
    result = processing.run('qgis:checkvalidity', {
        'INPUT_LAYER': input_layer,
        'METHOD': 2,  # Options: 0 - The one selected in digitizing settings, 1 - QGIS, 2 - GEOS
        'IGNORE_RING_SELF_INTERSECTION': False,
        'VALID_OUTPUT': valid_output_path,
        'INVALID_OUTPUT': invalid_output_path,
        'ERROR_OUTPUT': error_output_path
    })

    # Load the output layers into QGIS
    valid_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['VALID_OUTPUT'], 'Valid Features', 'ogr')
    invalid_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['INVALID_OUTPUT'], 'Invalid Features', 'ogr')
    error_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['ERROR_OUTPUT'], 'Error Points', 'ogr')
    
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(valid_layer)
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(invalid_layer)
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(error_layer)

# Execute the function
check_layer_validity()

brief_description: Calculates the total climb and descent along line geometries
full_description:
Calculates the total climb and descent along line geometries. The input layer must have Z values present. If Z values are not available, the Drape (set Z value from raster) algorithm may be used to add Z values from a DEM layer.
The output layer is a copy of the input layer with additional fields that contain the total climb ( climb ), total descent ( descent ), the minimum elevation ( minelev ) and the maximum elevation ( maxelev ) for each line geometry. If the input layer contains fields with the same names as these added fields, they will be renamed (field names will be altered to “name_2”, “name_3”, etc, finding the first non-duplicate name).

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Line layer
parameters.description: Line layer to calculate the climb for. Must have Z values.

parameters.label: Climb layer
parameters.description: Specification of the output (line) layer. One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def define_current_projection():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    # Load the shapefile as a vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Unprojected Layer', 'ogr')

    # Run the Define Current Projection algorithm
    result = processing.run('qgis:definecurrentprojection', {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'CRS': 'EPSG:4326',
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    })

    # Load the reprojected layer to QGIS
    projected_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Projected Layer', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(projected_layer)

# Execute the function
define_current_projection()

Calculates for point features distances to their nearest features in the same layer or in another layer.

original_code_example:
# Import necessary modules
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def generate_distance_matrix():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    target_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    # Load the shapefile as a vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Points', 'ogr')
    target_layer = QgsVectorLayer(target_path, 'Target Points', 'ogr')

    # Run the Distance Matrix algorithm
    result = processing.run('qgis:distancematrix', {
        'INPUT': input_path,
        'INPUT_FIELD': 'input_id',
        'TARGET': target_path,
        'TARGET_FIELD': 'target_id',
        'MATRIX_TYPE': 0, # Options: [0: 'Linear (N*k x 3) distance matrix', 1: 'Standard (N x T) distance matrix', 2: 'Summary distance matrix (mean, std. dev., min, max)']
        'NEAREST_POINTS': 0,
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    })

    # Load the distance matrix layer to QGIS
    distance_matrix_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Distance Matrix', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(distance_matrix_layer)

# Execute the function
generate_distance_matrix()

brief_description: Combines selected polygons of the input layer with certain adjacent polygons by erasing their common boundary
full_description:
Combines selected polygons of the input layer with certain adjacent polygons by erasing their common boundary. The adjacent polygon can be either the one with the largest or smallest area or the one sharing the largest common boundary with the polygon to be eliminated.
Eliminate is normally used to get rid of sliver polygons, i.e. tiny polygons that are a result of polygon intersection processes where boundaries of the inputs are similar but not identical.
Default menu: Vector ► Geoprocessing Tools

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: Input polygon vector layer.

parameters.name: MODE
parameters.label: Merge selection with the neighboring polygon with the
parameters.description: Choose the parameter to use in order to get rid ofthe selected polygons: 0 — Largest Area 1 — Smallest Area 2 — Largest Common Boundary.

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Eliminated
parameters.description: Specify the output vector layer. One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

def eliminate_selected_polygons():

brief_description: Runs a simple or complex query based only on SELECT with SQL syntax on the source layer.
full_description:
Runs a simple or complex query based only on SELECT with SQL syntax on the source layer.
Input datasources are identified with input1 , input2 … inputN and a simple query will look like SELECT*FROMinput1 .
Beside a simple query, you can add expressions or variables within the SQLquery parameter itself. This is particulary useful if this algorithm is executed within a Processing model and you want to use a model input as a parameter of the query. An example of a query will then be SELECT*FROM[%@table%] where @table is the variable that identifies the model input.
The result of the query will be added as a new layer.

parameters.name: INPUT_DATASOURCES
parameters.label: Additional input datasources (called input1,
parameters.description: ., inputN in the query). List of layers to query. In the SQL editor you canrefer these layers with their real name or alsowith input1 , input2 , inputN dependingon how many layers have been chosen..

parameters.name: INPUT_QUERY
parameters.label: SQL query
parameters.description: Type the string of your SQL query, e.g. SELECT * FROM input1 ..

parameters.name: INPUT_UID_FIELD
parameters.label: Unique identifier field Optional
parameters.description: Specify the column with unique ID.

parameters.name: INPUT_GEOMETRY_FIELD
parameters.label: Geometry field Optional
parameters.description: Specify the geometry field.

parameters.name: INPUT_GEOMETRY_TYPE
parameters.label: Geometry type Optional
parameters.description: Choose the geometry of the result.By default the algorithm will autodetect it.One of: 0 — Autodetect 1 — No geometry 2 — Point 3 — LineString 4 — Polygon 5 — MultiPoint 6 — MultiLineString 7 — MultiPolygon.

parameters.name: INPUT_GEOMETRY_CRS
parameters.label: CRS Optional
parameters.description: The CRS to assign to the output layer.

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: SQL Output
parameters.description: Specify the output layer created by the query. One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

import processing
from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer

def execute_sql_example():
    input_layer1_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    input_layer2_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    input_layer1 = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer1_path, """input1", "ogr")
    input_layer2 = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer2_path, "input2", "ogr")

    parameters = {
        'INPUT_DATASOURCES': [input_layer1, input_layer2],                                         
        'INPUT_QUERY': 'SELECT * FROM input1 WHERE input1.field1 > 100',                    
        'INPUT_UID_FIELD': 'id',                   
        'INPUT_GEOMETRY_FIELD': 'geometry',                  
        'INPUT_GEOMETRY_TYPE': 0,              
                                                  
                                            
                                                 
                                              
                                                 
                                                      
                                                   
        'INPUT_GEOMETRY_CRS': 'EPSG:4326',                               
        'OUTPUT': output_path               
    }

    result = processing.run("qgis:executesql", parameters)
    
    output_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'SQL Output', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_layer)

                      
execute_sql_example()

brief_description: Creates a shortlist of candidate coordinate reference systems, for instance for a layer with an unknown projection.
full_description:
Creates a shortlist of candidate coordinate reference systems, for instance for a layer with an unknown projection.
The area that the layer is expected to cover must be specified via the target area parameter. The coordinate reference system for this target area must be known to QGIS.
The algorithm operates by testing the layer’s extent in every known reference system and then listing any for which the bounds would be near the target area if the layer was in this projection.

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input Layer
parameters.description: Layer with unknown projection.

parameters.name: TARGET_AREA
parameters.label: Target area for layer (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
parameters.description: The area that the layer covers. Available methods are: Calculate from layer…: uses extent of a layer loaded in the current project Calculate from layout map…: uses extent of a layout map item in the active project Calculate from bookmark…: uses extent of a saved bookmark Use map canvas extent Draw o

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: CRS candidates
parameters.description: Specify the table (geometryless layer) for the CRSsuggestions (EPSG codes). One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing    

def find_projection():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'  # Ensure this is a path to a CSV file for output.

    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Unknown Projection Layer', 'ogr')
    
    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'TARGET_AREA': 'xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax',  # You can dynamically calculate or input the bounds here as needed.
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    
    result = processing.run('qgis:findprojection', parameters)
    
    crs_candidates = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'CRS Suggestions', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(crs_candidates)

find_projection()

brief_description: Creates a density (heatmap) raster of an input point vector layer using kernel density estimation
full_description:
Creates a density (heatmap) raster of an input point vector layer using kernel density estimation. Heatmaps allow easy identification of hotspots and clustering of points.
   The density is calculated based on the number of points in a location, with larger numbers of clustered points resulting in larger values.

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Point vector layer to use for the heatmap
parameters.description: Point vector layer to use for the heatmap

parameters.name: RADIUS
parameters.label: Heatmap search radius (or kernel bandwidth) in map units
parameters.description: Heatmap search radius (or kernel bandwidth) in map units. The radius specifies the distance around a point at which the influence of the point will be felt. Larger values result in greater smoothing, but smaller values may show finer details and variation in point density.

parameters.name: RADIUS_FIELD
parameters.label: Radius from field (optional
parameters.description: Radius from field (optional. Sets the search radius for each feature from an attribute field in the input layer.

parameters.name: PIXEL_SIZE
parameters.label: Output raster size
parameters.description: Output raster size. Pixel size of the output raster layer in layer units. Default is 0.1

parameters.name: WEIGHT_FIELD
parameters.label: Weight from field (Optional)
parameters.description: Weight from field (Optional)

parameters.name: KERNEL
parameters.label: Kernel shape
parameters.description: Kernel shape. Available values - 0: Quartic, 1: Triangular, 2: Uniform, 3: Triweight, 4: Epanechnikov

parameters.name: DECAY
parameters.label: Decay ratio (Triangular kernels only) - Optional
parameters.description: Decay ratio (Triangular kernels only) - Optional

parameters.name: OUTPUT_VALUE
parameters.label: Output value scaling
parameters.description: Output value scaling. Available values - 0: Raw, 1: Scaled

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Specify the output raster layer with kernel density values
parameters.description: Specify the output raster layer with kernel density values. One of: -Save to a Temporary File ('memory'), -Save to file...

brief_description: Calculates hypsometric curves for an input Digital Elevation Model
full_description:
Calculates hypsometric curves for an input Digital Elevation Model. Curves are produced as CSV files in an output folder specified by the user.
A hypsometric curve is a cumulative histogram of elevation values in a geographical area.
You can use hypsometric curves to detect differences in the landscape due to the geomorphology of the territory.

parameters.name: INPUT_DEM
parameters.label: DEM to analyze
parameters.description: Digital Terrain Model raster layer to use forcalculating altitudes.

parameters.name: BOUNDARY_LAYER
parameters.label: Boundary layer
parameters.description: Polygon vector layer with boundaries of areas usedto calculate hypsometric curves.

parameters.name: STEP
parameters.label: Step
parameters.description: Vertical distance between curves.

parameters.name: USE_PERCENTAGE
parameters.label: Use % of area instead of absolute value
parameters.description: Write area percentage to “Area” field of the CSV fileinstead of the absolute area.

parameters.name: OUTPUT_DIRECTORY
parameters.label: Hypsometric curves
parameters.description: Specify the output folder for the hypsometric curves.One of: Save to a Temporary Directory Save to Directory.

use_cases: DEM analysis for site suitability studies
Environmental modeling requiring slope/aspect inputs
Terrain visualization for client deliverables
Erosion risk and geohazard assessment
original_code_example:
# Import necessary modules
from qgis.core import QgsRasterLayer, QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def hypsometric_curves():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_dem_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    boundary_layer_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_directory_path = 'path/to/output'

    # Load the raster and vector layers
    input_dem = QgsRasterLayer(input_dem_path, 'DEM')
    boundary_layer = QgsVectorLayer(boundary_layer_path, 'Boundary', 'ogr')

    # Run the Hypsometric Curves algorithm
    result = processing.run('qgis:hypsometriccurves', {
        'INPUT_DEM': input_dem,
        'BOUNDARY_LAYER': boundary_layer,
        'STEP': 100.0,  # Default
        'USE_PERCENTAGE': False,  # Default
        'OUTPUT_DIRECTORY': output_directory_path
    })

# Execute the function
hypsometric_curves()

brief_description: Generates an Inverse Distance Weighted (IDW) interpolation of a point vector layer.
full_description:

Sample points are weighted during interpolation such that the influence of one point relative to another declines with distance from the unknown point you want to create.

The following elements are provided to compose the interpolation data: Vector layer [vector: any] and Interpolation attribute (i.e Attribute to use in the interpolation) [tablefield: numeric]. In the strig, the layer-field elements are separ

parameters.label: Sets the distance coefficient for the interpolation
parameters.description: Sets the distance coefficient for the interpolation. Minimum: 0.0, maximum: 100.0.

parameters.name: EXTENT
parameters.label: Extent of the output raster layer
parameters.description: Extent of the output raster layer. Available methods are: Calculate from layer…: uses extent of a layer loaded in the current project, Calculate from layout map…: uses extent of a layout map item in the active project, Calculate from bookmark…: uses extent of a saved bookmark, Use map canvas extent,

original_code_example:
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject, QgsRasterLayer, QgsCoordinateReferenceSystem
from PyQt5.QtCore import QVariant
import processing
def IDW_Interpolation(input_layer_path, attribute):
    # Load the input vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer_path, "Input Layer", "ogr")
	output_path = "path/to/input_file"

     #Determining the field index of the attribute
    fields = input_layer.fields()
    attribute_index = fields.indexOf(attribute) #obtaining the index of the the attribute to be interpolated

    #Define the parameters for IDW interpolation
    parameters = {
        'INTERPOLATION_DATA': f"{input_layer_path}::~::0::~::{attribute_index}::~::0",
        'DISTANCE_COEFFICIENT': 2,
        'EXTENT': input_layer.extent(),
        'PIXEL_SIZE': 0.1,
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    # Run the IDW interpolation algorithm
    processing.run("qgis:idwinterpolation", parameters)
    # Add the output raster layer to the QGIS project
    output_raster_layer = QgsRasterLayer(parameters['OUTPUT'], "IDW Interpolation")
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_raster_layer)

brief_description: Exports a vector layer to a SpatiaLite database
full_description: Exports a vector layer to a SpatiaLite database. Prior to this a connection between QGIS and the SpatiaLite database has to be created (see eg SpatiaLite Layers ).

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Layer to import
parameters.description: Vector layer to add to the database.

parameters.name: DATABASE
parameters.label: File database
parameters.description: The SQLite/SpatiaLite database file to connect to.

parameters.name: TABLENAME
parameters.label: Table to import to (leave blank to use layer name) Optional
parameters.description: Defines the table name for the imported vector file.If nothing is specified, the layer name will be used..

parameters.name: PRIMARY_KEY
parameters.label: Primary key field Optional
parameters.description: Use a field in the input vector layer as the primary key.

parameters.name: GEOMETRY_COLUMN
parameters.label: Geometry column
parameters.description: Defines the name of the geometry column in the newSpatiaLite table.Geometry information for the features is stored in thiscolumn..

parameters.name: ENCODING
parameters.label: Encoding Optional
parameters.description: Defines the encoding of the output layer.

parameters.name: OVERWRITE
parameters.label: Overwrite
parameters.description: If the specified table exists, setting this option to True will make sure that it is deleted and a newtable will be created before the features of the layer isadded.If this option is False and the table exists, thealgorithm will throw an exception (“table alreadyexists”)..

parameters.name: CREATEINDEX
parameters.label: Create spatial index
parameters.description: Specifies whether to create a spatial index or not.

parameters.name: LOWERCASE_NAMES
parameters.label: Convert field names to lowercase
parameters.description: Convert the field names of the input vector layerto lowercase.

parameters.name: DROP_STRING_LENGTH
parameters.label: Drop length constraint on character fields
parameters.description: Should length constraints on character fields bedropped or not.

parameters.name: FORCE_SINGLEPART
parameters.label: Create single-part geometries instead of multi-part
parameters.description: Should the features of the output layer besingle-part instead of multi-part.By default the existing geometries informationare preserved..

from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer
import processing

def import_to_spatialite():
    input_layer_path = """path/to/input_file"
    database_path = "D:/Data/spatialite_database.sqlite"
    output_path = "path/to/input_file"

    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer_path, "Input Layer", "ogr")

    params = {
        'INPUT': input_layer_path,
        'DATABASE': database_path,
        'TABLENAME': '',                                  
        'PRIMARY_KEY': '',                                         
        'GEOMETRY_COLUMN': '',                     
        'ENCODING': '',                      
        'OVERWRITE': '',                   
        'CREATEINDEX': '',                   
        'LOWERCASE_NAMES': '',                   
        'DROP_STRING_LENGTH': '',                    
        'FORCE_SINGLEPART': '',                    
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }

    result = processing.run("qgis:importintospatialite", params)
    output_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Imported_output', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_layer)

import_to_spatialite()

import processing
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject

def lines_to_polygons():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'  # path to the input line shapefile
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'  # path for the output polygons file 

    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Layer', 'ogr')

    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_path, 
        'OUTPUT': output_path  # Options: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ), Save to File…, Save to Geopackage…, Save to Database Table…, Append to Layer…
    }

    result = processing.run('qgis:linestopolygons', parameters)

    output_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Polygons', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_layer)

lines_to_polygons()

from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def list_unique_values():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    html_report_path = 'path/to/output'

    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Layer', 'ogr')

    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'FIELDS': ['field_name'],  # Specify the field(s) you want to analyze
        'OUTPUT': output_path,
        'OUTPUT_HTML_FILE': html_report_path,
    }

    result = processing.run('qgis:listuniquevalues', parameters)

    summary_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Unique Values', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(summary_layer)

list_unique_values()

import processing
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject

def generate_plot():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/output'

    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Table', 'ogr')

    result = processing.run('qgis:meanandstandarddeviationplot', {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'NAME_FIELD': 'CategoryField',  # Specify the field name for categories
        'VALUE_FIELD': 'ValueField',  # Specify the field name for values

    })

    print(f"""Plot generated: {result['OUTPUT']}")

generate_plot()

brief_description: Creates geometries which enclose the features from an input layer
full_description:
Creates geometries which enclose the features from an input layer. The features can be grouped by a field. The output layer will then contain one feature per group value with a geometry (MBB) that covers the geometries of the features with matching value.
The following enclosing geometry types are supported:

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: Input vector layer.

parameters.name: FIELD
parameters.label: Field Optional
parameters.description: Features can be grouped by a field.If set, this causes the output layer to contain one feature pergrouped value with a minimal geometry covering only thefeatures with matching values..

parameters.name: TYPE
parameters.label: Geometry type
parameters.description: Enclosing geometry types. One of: 0 — Envelope (Bounding Box) 1 — Minimum Oriented Rectangle 2 — Minimum Enclosing Circle 3 — Convex Hull.

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Bounding geometry
parameters.description: Specify the output polygon vector layer. One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

Terrain visualization for client deliverables

from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def generate_bounding_geometry():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Layer', 'ogr')

    result = processing.run('qgis:minimumboundinggeometry', {
        'INPUT': input_path,
        'FIELD': None,  # Optional: Specify a field for grouping
        'TYPE': 0,  # 0 — Envelope (Bounding Box), 1 — Minimum Oriented Rectangle, 2 — Minimum Enclosing Circle, 3 — Convex Hull
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    })

    bounding_geometry_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Bounding Geometry', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(bounding_geometry_layer)

# Execute the function
generate_bounding_geometry()

Unique

import processing
from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer

def execute_and_load_sql():
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'

    # Define the parameters
    parameters = {
        'DATABASE': 'my_database_connection',  # specify the existing connection name
        'SQL': 'SELECT * FROM my_table',
        'ID_FIELD': 'id',
        'GEOMETRY_FIELD': '',
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }

    # Run the PostGIS execute and load SQL command
    result = processing.run("""qgis:postgisexecuteandloadsql", parameters)

                              
    result_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'SQL_Result', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(result_layer)

                      
execute_and_load_sql()

brief_description: Takes a vector layer and generates a new one that contains only a subset of the features in the input layer.
full_description:
Takes a vector layer and generates a new one that contains only a subset of the features in the input layer.
The subset is defined randomly, based on feature IDs, using a percentage or count value to define the total number of features in the subset. The percentage/count value is not applied to the whole layer, but instead to each category. Categories are defined according to a given attribute.

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: Vector layer to select the features from.

parameters.name: FIELD
parameters.label: ID field
parameters.description: Category of the source vector layer to select the features from.

parameters.name: METHOD
parameters.label: Method
parameters.description: Random selection method. One of: 0 — Number of selected features 1 — Percentage of selected features.

parameters.name: NUMBER
parameters.label: Number/percentage of selected features
parameters.description: Number or percentage of features to select.

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Extracted (random stratified)
parameters.description: Specify the output vector layer for the randomlyselected features.One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

# Import necessary modules
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def generate_random_points():
    # Define the input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_dir = 'path/to/output'
    output_file_name = """random_points_output.shp"
    output_path = f"{output_dir}/{output_file_name}"
    
                          
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, "Input_Polygon", "ogr")
    
                                             
    random_points_params = {
        'INPUT': input_path,
        'STRATEGY': 0,                                                 
        'VALUE': 1.0,
        'MIN_DISTANCE': 0.0,
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    
                                                 
    result = processing.run("qgis:randompointsinsidepolygons", random_points_params)
    
                                            
    random_points_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Random_Points', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(random_points_layer)

                      
generate_random_points()

The subset is defined randomly, based on feature IDs, using a percentage or count value to define the total number of features in the subset.

original_code_example:
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def random_selection():
    input_path = 'path/to/your/input_layer.shp'
    output_path = 'path/to/save/selected_features.shp'
    
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, """Input Layer", "ogr")
    
    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_path,
        'METHOD': 0,                                                                        
        'NUMBER': 10,
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    
    result = processing.run("qgis:randomselection", parameters)
    
    selected_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Randomly_Selected_Features', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(selected_layer)

random_selection()

brief_description: Takes a vector layer and selects a subset of its features
full_description:
Takes a vector layer and selects a subset of its features. No new layer is generated by this algorithm.
The subset is defined randomly, based on feature IDs, using a percentage or count value to define the total number of features in the subset.
The percentage/count value is not applied to the whole layer, but instead to each category.
Categories are defined according to a given attribute, which is also specified as an input parameter for the algorithm.
No new outputs are created.
Default menu: Vector ► Research Tools

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: Vector layer to select features in.

parameters.name: FIELD
parameters.label: ID field
parameters.description: Category of the input layer to select the features from.

parameters.name: METHOD
parameters.label: Method
parameters.description: Random selection method. One of: 0 — Number of selected features 1 — Percentage of selected features.

parameters.name: NUMBER
parameters.label: Number/percentage of selected features
parameters.description: Number or percentage of features to select.

brief_description: Creates a new point layer with its points placed in a regular grid within a given extent.
full_description:
Creates a new point layer with its points placed in a regular grid within a given extent.
The grid is specified either by the spacing between the points (same spacing for all dimensions) or by the number of points to generate. In the latter case, the spacing will be determined from the extent. In order to generate a full rectangular grid, at least the number of points specified by the user is generated for the latter case.
Random offsets to the point spacing can be applied, resulting in a non-regular point pattern.
Default menu: Vector ► Research Tools

parameters.name: EXTENT
parameters.label: Input extent (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
parameters.description: Map extent for the random points Available methods are: Calculate from layer…: uses extent of a layer loaded in the current project Calculate from layout map…: uses extent of a layout map item in the active project Calculate from bookmark…: uses extent of a saved bookmark Use map canvas extent Draw

parameters.name: SPACING
parameters.label: Point spacing/count
parameters.description: Spacing between the points, or the number of points, dependingon whether Use point spacing is checked or not..

parameters.name: INSET
parameters.label:
Initial inset from corner 
(LH side)
parameters.description: Offsets the points relative to the upper left corner.The value is used for both the X and Y axis..

parameters.name: RANDOMIZE
parameters.label: Apply random offset to point spacing
parameters.description: If checked the points will have a random spacing.

parameters.name: IS_SPACING
parameters.label: Use point spacing
parameters.description: If unchecked the point spacing is not taken into account.

parameters.name: CRS
parameters.label:
Output layer 
CRS
parameters.description: CRS of the random points layer.

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Regular points
parameters.description: Specify the output regular point layer. One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

use_cases: Zonal summarization of raster values within polygons
Spatial autocorrelation and clustering analysis
Population-weighted demographic aggregation
Environmental exposure assessment by census unit

from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer
import processing

def create_regular_points():
    # Define output path
    output_path = """path/to/input_file"

                                                  
    parameters = {
        'EXTENT': 'xmin,xmax,ymin,ymax',                                                                                                                  
        'SPACING': 100,                 
        'INSET': 0.0,                 
        'RANDOMIZE': False,                   
        'IS_SPACING': True,                  
        'CRS': 'Project CRS',                         
        'OUTPUT': output_path                                    
    }

                                      
    result = processing.run("qgis:regularpoints", parameters)

    from qgis.core import QgsProject,QgsVectorLayer
    def select_by_attribute(input_layer_path):
        # Define the parameters
        input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer_path, "Input Layer", "ogr")

        # Define the parameters Example below:
        field_name = 
        parameters = {
            'INPUT': input_layer_path,
            'FIELD': 'Population',
            'OPERATOR': 4, # Select the appropriate operator based on the task. Many different operators are available:0 — =,1 — ≠, 2 — >, 3 — >=, 4 — <, 5 — <=, 6 — begins with, 7 — contains, 8 — is null, 9 — is not null, 10 — does not contain
            'VALUE': 3000,
			'METHOD': 0,
            'OUTPUT': output_layer_path
        }
        # Perform the extract by attribute operation
        result = processing.run("qgis:selectbyattribute", parameters)
        # Load the selected features as a new layer
        output_layer = QgsVectorLayer(output_path, 'Population_less_than_3000', 'ogr')
        QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_layer)
    input_layer_path = "path/to/input_file"  # path to the input shapefile

    from qgis.core import QgsProject,QgsVectorLayer
    def extract_by_expression(input_layer_path):
        # Define the parameters
        input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer_path, "Input Layer", "ogr")
    
        # Define the parameters Example below:
        parameters = {
            'INPUT': input_layer_path,
            'EXPRESSION':' "STATEFP"  =\'42\' AND  "Obesity" <30',
            'OUTPUT': output_layer_path
        }
        # Perform the extract by attribute operation
        result = processing.run("qgis:selectbyexpression", parameters)
        # Load the selected features as a new layer

        QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(output_layer)
    input_layer_path = "path/to/input_file"  # path to the input shapefile

    select_by_attribute(input_layer_path)

import processing

def calculate_statistics_by_category(input_layer_path, output_path):
    # Load the vector layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_layer_path, """Input Layer", "ogr")

        'VALUES_FIELD_NAME': 'value_field',            
        'CATEGORIES_FIELD_NAME': ['category_field1', 'category_field2'],

    }

                                                
    result = processing.run('qgis:statisticsbycategories', parameters)

brief_description: Generates a Triangulated Irregular Network (TIN) interpolation of a point vector layer.
full_description:
Generates a Triangulated Irregular Network (TIN) interpolation of a point vector layer.
With the TIN method you can create a surface formed by triangles of nearest neighbor points. To do this, circumcircles around selected sample points are created and their intersections are connected to a network of non overlapping and as compact as possible triangles. The resulting surfaces are not smooth.
The algorithm creates both the raster layer of the interpolated values and the vector line layer with the triangulation boundaries.

parameters.name: INTERPOLATION_DATA
parameters.label: Input layer(s)

use_cases: Creating continuous surfaces from point sampling data
Spatial interpolation of environmental measurements
Filling data gaps in meteorological or soil datasets
Generating smooth raster outputs from irregular point data
original_code_example:
import processing
from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer

def tin_interpolation():
    # Define input and output paths
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    
    # Load the input layer
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Points', 'ogr')
    
    # Define the interpolation data string
    interpolation_data = '{}::~::Elevation::~::0::~::Points'.format(input_path)
    
    # Define parameters
    parameters = {
        'INTERPOLATION_DATA': interpolation_data,
        'METHOD': 0,  # Options: [0: 'Linear', 1: 'Clough-Toucher (cubic)']
        'EXTENT': 'xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax',  # Provide actual values according to dataset
        'PIXEL_SIZE': 0.1,
        'OUTPUT': output_path,
        'TRIANGULATION': 'Skip Output'  # Options: ['Skip Output', 'TEMPORARY_OUTPUT', 'Save to File…', 'Save to Geopackage…', 'Save to Database Table…']
    }
    
    # Perform the TIN interpolation
    result = processing.run('qgis:tininterpolation', parameters)
    
    # Load the interpolated raster layer to QGIS
    interpolated_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'Interpolated Raster', 'gdal')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(interpolated_layer)

# Execute the function
tin_interpolation()

brief_description: Assigns a color index to polygon features in such a way that no adjacent polygons share the same color index, whilst minimizing the number of colors required.
full_description:
Assigns a color index to polygon features in such a way that no adjacent polygons share the same color index, whilst minimizing the number of colors required.
The algorithm allows choice of method to use when assigning colors.
A minimum number of colors can be specified if desired. The color index is saved to a new attribute named color_id.
The following example shows the algorithm with four different colors chosen; as you can see each color class has the same amount of features.

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: The input polygon layer.

parameters.name: MIN_COLORS
parameters.label: Minimum number of colors
parameters.description: The minimum number of colors to assign.Minimum 1, maximum 1000..

parameters.name: MIN_DISTANCE
parameters.label: Minimum distance between features
parameters.description: Prevent nearby (but non-touching) features frombeing assigned equal colors.Minimum 0.0..

parameters.name: BALANCE
parameters.label: Balance color assignment
parameters.description: Options are: 0 — By feature count Attempts to assign colors so that the count offeatures assigned to each individual color indexis balanced. 1 — By assigned area Assigns colors so that the total area of featuresassigned to each color is balanced.This mode can be useful to help avoid largefeatures re

parameters.name: OUTPUT
parameters.label: Colored
parameters.description: Specify the output layer. One of: Create Temporary Layer ( TEMPORARY_OUTPUT ) Save to File… Save to Geopackage… Save to Database Table… The file encoding can also be changed here..

import processing
from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject

def topological_coloring():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_dir = 'path/to/output'
    output_file_name = 'Colored_Output.shp'
    output_path = f"""{output_dir}/{output_file_name}"
    
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, "InputPolygon", "ogr")
    
    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_path,
        'MIN_COLORS': 4,
        'MIN_DISTANCE': 0.0,
        'BALANCE': 0,                                                                                         
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    
    result = processing.run("qgis:topologicalcoloring", parameters)
    
    color_layer = QgsVectorLayer(result['OUTPUT'], 'ColoredOutput', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(color_layer)

topological_coloring()

brief_description: Computes a buffer area for all the features in an input layer.
full_description:
Computes a buffer area for all the features in an input layer.
The size of the buffer for a given feature is defined by an attribute, so it allows different features to have different buffer sizes.

parameters.name: INPUT
parameters.label: Input layer
parameters.description: Input vector layer.

parameters.name: DISTANCE
parameters.label: Distance field
parameters.description: Attribute for the distance radius of the buffer.

parameters.name: SEGMENTS
parameters.label: Segments
parameters.description: Controls the number of line segments to use to approximate aquarter circle when creating rounded offsets..

parameters.name: DISSOLVE
parameters.label: Dissolve result
parameters.description: Choose to dissolve the final buffer, resulting in a singlefeature covering all input features. Fig. 28.8 Normal and dissolved buffer .

parameters.name: END_CAP_STYLE
parameters.label: End cap style
parameters.description: Controls how line endings are handled in the buffer. Fig. 28.9 Round, flat and square cap styles .

parameters.name: JOIN_STYLE
parameters.label: Join style
parameters.description: Specifies whether round, miter or beveled joins should be usedwhen offsetting corners in a line..

parameters.name: MITER_LIMIT
parameters.label: Miter limit
parameters.description: Only applicable for mitered join styles, and controls themaximum distance from the offset curve to use when creating amitered join..

import processing
from qgis.core import QgsProject, QgsVectorLayer

def variable_distance_buffer():
    input_path = """path/to/input_file"
    output_path = "path/to/input_file"
    
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, "Input Layer", "ogr")
    
    buffer_params = {
        'INPUT': input_path,
        'DISTANCE': 'buffer_field_name',                                                     
        'SEGMENTS': 5,
        'DISSOLVE': False,
        'END_CAP_STYLE': 0,                                         
        'JOIN_STYLE': 0,                                         
        'MITER_LIMIT': 2.0,
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }
    
    result = processing.run("qgis:variabledistancebuffer", buffer_params)
    
    buffered_layer = QgsVectorLayer(output_path, 'Buffered_output', 'ogr')
    QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(buffered_layer)

variable_distance_buffer()

from qgis.core import QgsVectorLayer, QgsProject
import processing

def create_histogram():
    input_path = 'path/to/input_file'
    output_path = '/path/to/output.html'
  
    input_layer = QgsVectorLayer(input_path, 'Input Layer', 'ogr')

    parameters = {
        'INPUT': input_layer,
        'FIELD': 'Attribute',  # Replace with the appropriate attribute name
        'BINS': 10,  # Default number of bins
        'OUTPUT': output_path
    }

    result = processing.run("""qgis:vectorlayerhistogram", parameters)

create_histogram()
